From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Australian film director (1921–2011)

For other people named Don Sharp, see [Don Sharp (disambiguation)](/wiki/Don_Sharp_(disambiguation) "Don Sharp (disambiguation)").

| Don Sharp | |
| --- | --- |
| Filming *[The Four Feathers](/wiki/The_Four_Feathers_(1978_film) "The Four Feathers (1978 film)")* (1978) | |
| Born | Donald Herman Sharp  (1921-04-19)19 April 1921  [Hobart](/wiki/Hobart "Hobart"), [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania"), Australia |
| Died | 14 December 2011(2011-12-14) (aged 90)  [Wadebridge](/wiki/Wadebridge "Wadebridge"), [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall"), England |
| Occupations | Director, writer, actor |
| Known for | [Kiss of the Vampire](/wiki/Kiss_of_the_Vampire_(film) "Kiss of the Vampire (film)") (1963), [Rasputin, the Mad Monk](/wiki/Rasputin,_the_Mad_Monk "Rasputin, the Mad Monk") (1966) |

**Donald Herman Sharp** (19 April 1921 – 14 December 2011) was an Australian film director.

His best known films were made for [Hammer](/wiki/Hammer_Film_Productions "Hammer Film Productions") in the 1960s, and included *[Kiss of the Vampire](/wiki/Kiss_of_the_Vampire_(film) "Kiss of the Vampire (film)")* (1963) and *[Rasputin, the Mad Monk](/wiki/Rasputin,_the_Mad_Monk "Rasputin, the Mad Monk")* (1966). In 1965 he directed *[The Face of Fu Manchu](/wiki/The_Face_of_Fu_Manchu "The Face of Fu Manchu")*, based on the character created by [Sax Rohmer](/wiki/Sax_Rohmer "Sax Rohmer"), and starring [Christopher Lee](/wiki/Christopher_Lee "Christopher Lee"). Sharp also directed the sequel *[The Brides of Fu Manchu](/wiki/The_Brides_of_Fu_Manchu "The Brides of Fu Manchu")* (1966). In the 1980s he was also responsible for several hugely popular miniseries adapted from the novels of [Barbara Taylor Bradford](/wiki/Barbara_Taylor_Bradford "Barbara Taylor Bradford").

## Early career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Early career")]

### Early life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early life")]

Sharp was born in Hobart, Tasmania, in 1921, according to official military records and his own account (some sources still give 1922 as his year of birth). He was the second of four children.

He attended [St Virgil's College](/wiki/St_Virgil%27s_College "St Virgil's College") and began appearing regularly in theatre productions at the Playhouse Theatre in Hobart, where he trained under a young [Stanley Burbury](/wiki/Stanley_Burbury "Stanley Burbury").[[1]](#cite_note-1) He later said this was prompted "by a desire not to study to become an accountant, which is what my parents wanted for me."[[2]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_14-2) Among the plays Sharp appeared in were *[You Can't Take It With You](/wiki/You_Can%27t_Take_It_with_You_(play) "You Can't Take It with You (play)")* and *[Our Town](/wiki/Our_Town "Our Town")*. He also directed a production of *[Stage Door](/wiki/Stage_Door_(play) "Stage Door (play)")*.[[3]](#cite_note-ind-3) He studied accountancy in the evenings but this was interrupted by war service.[[2]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_14-2)

### War service

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: War service")]

Sharp enlisted in the [Royal Australian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Australian_Air_Force "Royal Australian Air Force") on 7 April 1941 and was transferred to Singapore. In addition to his military duties he appeared in radio and on stage with a touring English company. Among his radio performances were *[Escape](/wiki/Escape_(play) "Escape (play)")* and *[The Barretts of Wimpole Street](/wiki/The_Barretts_of_Wimpole_Street "The Barretts of Wimpole Street")*. "The acting bug had definitely gotten hold of me," says Sharp, "and I did a bit of it while I was in the RAAF as well, in the odd moment."[[2]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_14-2)

Sharp was invalided out before the city fell to the Japanese. He returned to Melbourne and recuperated at Heidelberg Hospital. Sharp spent the majority of his war service in Melbourne, appearing in amateur theatre productions of *[Quality Street](/wiki/Quality_Street_(play) "Quality Street (play)")* and *[The Late Christopher Bean](/wiki/The_Late_Christopher_Bean "The Late Christopher Bean")* as well as recorded broadcasts and ABC plays.

In early 1943 Sharo moved to Hobart. He appeared in a theatre production of *[Interval](/wiki/Interval_(play) "Interval (play)")* by [Sumner Locke Elliott](/wiki/Sumner_Locke_Elliott "Sumner Locke Elliott"), also serving as assistant director.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) Following this he appeared in a theatre revue, *[Khaki Kapers](/wiki/Khaki_Kapers "Khaki Kapers")*, notably in a sketch which figured a flag flown over the air force station in Singapore which Sharp had brought back with him.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Sharp was discharged from the air force on 17 March 1944 at the rank of corporal.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-hobart-8)

### Acting career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Acting career")]

After the war Sharp did not want to return to Hobart. He auditioned for and won an understudy's position in [J. C. Williamson Limited](/wiki/J._C._Williamson_Limited "J. C. Williamson Limited") version of the Broadway comedy *[Kiss and Tell](/wiki/Kiss_and_Tell_(play) "Kiss and Tell (play)")*; when a bout of laryngitis incapacitated one of the leads two weeks later, Sharp stepped into the role. He toured in the production from 1944-1945 then went on to appear in such plays as *[Arsenic and Old Lace](/wiki/Arsenic_and_Old_Lace_(play) "Arsenic and Old Lace (play)")* (1945) and *[The Dancing Years](/wiki/The_Dancing_Years "The Dancing Years")*. He worked for [Morris West](/wiki/Morris_West "Morris West")'s production company in radio and played a small role in *[Smithy](/wiki/Smithy_(1946_film) "Smithy (1946 film)")* (1946), one of the few feature films shot in Australia at this time.[[9]](#cite_note-FILM_NOTES-9)

Sharp also toured Japan performing for the occupying troops there. From Japan he went to London in 1948. "I could have gone on with a theatrical
career in Australia," says Sharp, "but what I really wanted was movies. So I went to England."[[2]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_14-2)

## Move to England

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Move to England")]

### *Ha'Penny Breeze*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ha'Penny Breeze")]

Arriving in England in 1948, Sharp got some stage work quickly "but I couldn't even get an appointment to see a casting director" for films.[[2]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_14-2)

He was sharing a flat with an assistant director and they decided to make their own film. He co-wrote *[Ha'penny Breeze](/wiki/Ha%27penny_Breeze "Ha'penny Breeze")* (1950), with a fellow Australian, [Frank Worth](/wiki/Frank_Worth "Frank Worth"). Together with another man, [Darcy Conyers](/wiki/Darcy_Conyers "Darcy Conyers"), they formed a production company and raised finance to make the £8,000 film. Sharp also played a leading role, did the accounts and helped with the direction. The film was not a large hit but it was theatrically released.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Sharp also got a small role in a British radio adaptation of *[Robbery Under Arms](/wiki/Robbery_Under_Arms "Robbery Under Arms")* (1950).[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Sharp said "Shortly after, a number of influential film people made contact with me, but none of them offered me a job as an actor — they all asked if I would write for them!"[[14]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_15-14) Sharp was unable to cash in on *Ha'penny Breeze* as he came down with a recurrence of tuberculosis and spent nearly two years in hospital, during which he had six ribs and one lung removed.[[15]](#cite_note-dontwo-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

### Group 3

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Group 3")]

When Sharp recovered he got some acting roles in such films as *[The Planter's Wife](/wiki/The_Planter%27s_Wife_(1952_film) "The Planter's Wife (1952 film)")* (1952), *[Appointment in London](/wiki/Appointment_in_London "Appointment in London")* (1953), *[The Cruel Sea](/wiki/The_Cruel_Sea_(1953_film) "The Cruel Sea (1953 film)")* (1953) and *[You Know What Sailors Are](/wiki/You_Know_What_Sailors_Are_(1954_film) "You Know What Sailors Are (1954 film)")* (1954). Several of these films were directed by [Ken Annakin](/wiki/Ken_Annakin "Ken Annakin") who Sharp says was particularly helpful giving him jobs when needed.[[17]](#cite_note-donthree-17)

He began to turn increasingly to writing and directing.[[3]](#cite_note-ind-3) Sharp said his background as an actor was useful for his development as a director, in particular it developed his sense of timing:

> You’ve got to know, for example, a thing I was taught early in theatre – if there's a scene in a movie, in a play, that always gets good laughs, on a good night, when there's a good and laughing audience, you’ll get laughs in the build-up to it, in the five or ten minutes beforehand, because it's a good audience who's appreciative of what's going on. On a bad night, when the audience are not laughing, increase your pace, get them at the point. And this teaches you a control of speed and how to control an audience. . . . Working with good actors, you get a feeling of timing with them; although sometimes the timing between them can be good but their overall pace, which is quite different, can be wrong – its context in the film, because of the situation in the film, perhaps there should be that little more urgency, therefore pace, in the scene.[[18]](#cite_note-retro-18)

When Sharp was ill he received "messages of encouragement" from Michael Balcon, John Grierson and James Lawrie. When he got out of hospital he contacted them and they invited him to write an original story for Group Three, a new government-backed film company which had a brief to support new talent.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Sharp sold them an original script called *[Child's Play](/wiki/Child%27s_Play_(1954_film) "Child's Play (1954 film)")* (made 1952, released 1954). Group Three liked Sharp's work and assigned him to work on the script for *Background*; he was also given the job as assistant to the producer which he later called "the most wonderful education".[[15]](#cite_note-dontwo-15) He stayed with Group Three for two years, writing four films and working as a personal assistant to the producer on them, as well as (after three attempts) getting in the ACTT unnion. Sharp later said, "no doubt about it, *Ha'penny Breeze* paid off."[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Group Three bought a story of Sharp's, originally called *The Norfolk Story*.[[21]](#cite_note-21) He turned this into a novel called *[Conflict of Wings](/wiki/Conflict_of_Wings "Conflict of Wings")* (1954), the title under which it was filmed; Sharp also collaborated on the screenplay with [John Pudney](/wiki/John_Pudney "John Pudney"), and did some second unit directing.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Sharp and Pudney then wrote *[The Blue Peter](/wiki/The_Blue_Peter_(1955_film) "The Blue Peter (1955 film)")* (1955) for Group Three.[[23]](#cite_note-23) Once again, Sharp also directed second unit, and he began to develop ambitions to direct. Sharp was offered a job at [Ealing Studios](/wiki/Ealing_Studios "Ealing Studios") as a production assistant but decided to turn it down.[[15]](#cite_note-dontwo-15)

## Director

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Director")]

### Early films and documentaries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Early films and documentaries")]

Sharp left Group Three to direct some documentaries for Pathe. He worked on a proposed film at Ealing about the [Skeleton Coast](/wiki/Skeleton_Coast "Skeleton Coast") which was never made.[[15]](#cite_note-dontwo-15) Sharp's first feature as director was *[The Stolen Airliner](/wiki/The_Stolen_Airliner "The Stolen Airliner")* (1955), made for the [Children's Film Foundation](/wiki/Children%27s_Film_Foundation "Children's Film Foundation"), based on a script by Pudney.[[14]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_15-14)

Sharp then received an offer from the BBC to replace fellow Australian, [Bruce Beeby](/wiki/Bruce_Beeby "Bruce Beeby"), as an actor on the science fiction serial *[Journey into Space](/wiki/Journey_into_Space "Journey into Space")*. The show was recorded on Sundays, enabling Sharp to appear in it while still continuiing with his writing and directing work. Sharp was hired by [Ealing Studios](/wiki/Ealing_Studios "Ealing Studios") to adapt the novel *[Robbery Under Arms](/wiki/Robbery_Under_Arms "Robbery Under Arms")* into a feature film script. He says some of his work was included in the [final script](/wiki/Robbery_Under_Arms_(1957_film) "Robbery Under Arms (1957 film)") which was ultimately done by [Bill Lipscomb](/wiki/Bill_Lipscomb "Bill Lipscomb").[[15]](#cite_note-dontwo-15)

Sharp made some television documentary shows for Pathe. During the making of one of them, *Crossroads* (1955), a film about why British soldiers left the army, he met actor Mary Steele who he later married. Sharp directed a "three reeler" for [Warwick Films](/wiki/Warwick_Films "Warwick Films") in Rome called *Arrivederci Roma* (1956).[[15]](#cite_note-dontwo-15) This was followed by a documentary for Martin Films, *The Passing Years* (1957), a dramatised documentary about the British motor industry.[[15]](#cite_note-dontwo-15)

Sharp's second feature film as director as another for the Children's Film Foundation, *[The Adventures of Hal 5](/wiki/The_Adventures_of_Hal_5 "The Adventures of Hal 5")*. He received an offer to direct second unit on *[Carve Her Name with Pride](/wiki/Carve_Her_Name_with_Pride "Carve Her Name with Pride")* (1958), directed by Lewis Gilbert; Sharp was responsible for various action sequences.[[15]](#cite_note-dontwo-15)

Sharp wrote and directed *[The Golden Disc](/wiki/The_Golden_Disc "The Golden Disc")* (1959), starring his wife, Mary Steele. This was the first British rock 'n' roll movie – released a year before the [Cliff Richard](/wiki/Cliff_Richard "Cliff Richard") vehicle *[Expresso Bongo](/wiki/Expresso_Bongo "Expresso Bongo")* (1959) and a full two years ahead of *[Beat Girl](/wiki/Beat_Girl "Beat Girl")* (1960). Sharp was hired to do more second unit work, on *[Harry Black](/wiki/Harry_Black_(film) "Harry Black (film)")* (1958), which involved shooting tiger footage in India. After this he made a documentary for American television at [Expo 58](/wiki/Expo_58 "Expo 58"), and one for the British army called *Keeping the Peace* (1959).[[17]](#cite_note-donthree-17)

### Independent Artists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Independent Artists")]

After an unsuccessful attempt to get up finance for a film with [Lonnie Donegan](/wiki/Lonnie_Donegan "Lonnie Donegan") Sharp made two films for Independent Artists. The first was a low-budget thriller, *[The Professionals](/wiki/The_Professionals_(1960_film) "The Professionals (1960 film)")* (1960), which screened on US TV as part of the *[Kraft Mystery Theatre](/wiki/Kraft_Mystery_Theatre "Kraft Mystery Theatre")*.[[17]](#cite_note-donthree-17) The second was *[Linda](/wiki/Linda_(1960_film) "Linda (1960 film)")* (1960), a teen drama starring [Carol White](/wiki/Carol_White "Carol White") for [Independent Artists](/wiki/Independent_Artists "Independent Artists"), which went out as a support feature for *[Saturday Night and Sunday Morning](/wiki/Saturday_Night_and_Sunday_Morning "Saturday Night and Sunday Morning")* (1960) and is now considered a [lost film](/wiki/Lost_film "Lost film"). Sharp then made another army documentary.[[17]](#cite_note-donthree-17)

He went into TV, becoming the resident director for the first season of *[Ghost Squad](/wiki/Ghost_Squad_(TV_series) "Ghost Squad (TV series)")* (1961–62).[[24]](#cite_note-24) Sharp directed *[Two Guys Abroad](/wiki/Two_Guys_Abroad "Two Guys Abroad")* (1962) with [George Raft](/wiki/George_Raft "George Raft"), which was intended as a pilot for a TV series or as a B movie, but ended up not being released at all.[[17]](#cite_note-donthree-17) Sharp then directed second unit on *[The Fast Lady](/wiki/The_Fast_Lady "The Fast Lady")* (1962) for [Ken Annakin](/wiki/Ken_Annakin "Ken Annakin").

### Hammer Films and Harry Alan Towers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Hammer Films and Harry Alan Towers")]

Sharp received an offer from [Tony Hinds](/wiki/Tony_Hinds "Tony Hinds") of [Hammer Films](/wiki/Hammer_Films "Hammer Films") who had seen *The Professionals* and was looking for a director for Hammer's vampire movie *[Kiss of the Vampire](/wiki/Kiss_of_the_Vampire_(film) "Kiss of the Vampire (film)")* (1963). Sharp had never seen a horror movie before but agreed after watching several Hammer films.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[14]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_15-14) According to his obituary Sharp helped make an "atmospheric, suspenseful gothic horror and giving a depth to the characters that was sometimes missing in Hammer's other vampire productions."[[3]](#cite_note-ind-3) *Kiss of the Vampire* is now one of Hammer's most highly regarded horrors; Sharp's *New York Times* obituary says "Not a few Hammer fans contend that *Kiss of the Vampire* is one of the greatest Gothic horror movies ever made".[[26]](#cite_note-26)

*Kiss of the Vampire* was shot in 1962. After making it, Sharp went back to television, directing episodes of *[The Human Jungle](/wiki/The_Human_Jungle_(TV_series) "The Human Jungle (TV series)")*, then made another teen musical in the vein of *The Golden Disc*, *[It's All Happening](/wiki/It%27s_All_Happening_(film) "It's All Happening (film)")* (1963), with [Tommy Steele](/wiki/Tommy_Steele "Tommy Steele").[[27]](#cite_note-27)

He returned to Hammer for a swashbuckler, *[The Devil-Ship Pirates](/wiki/The_Devil-Ship_Pirates "The Devil-Ship Pirates")* (1964) which starred Christopher Lee, who would make several movies with Sharp.[[28]](#cite_note-Koetting_p_11-28) By now *Kiss of the Vampire* had been released, and Sharp started receiving offers to direct more horror films; he says [Milton Subotsky](/wiki/Milton_Subotsky "Milton Subotsky") offered him the choice of three scripts but Sharp liked none of them.[[29]](#cite_note-donfour-29) Instead Sharp made *[Witchcraft](/wiki/Witchcraft_(1964_film) "Witchcraft (1964 film)")* (1964), for producer [Robert L. Lippert](/wiki/Robert_L._Lippert "Robert L. Lippert"). Sharp called it "a little four-week movie, very quickly done, but it received some lovely notices".[[14]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_15-14)

Sharp then spent several months directing second unit on *[Those Magnificent Men in Their Flying Machines](/wiki/Those_Magnificent_Men_in_Their_Flying_Machines "Those Magnificent Men in Their Flying Machines")* (1965) at the behest of director [Ken Annakin](/wiki/Ken_Annakin "Ken Annakin").[[28]](#cite_note-Koetting_p_11-28) Sharp said "I had to think very hard about going back to second unit after directing a half-dozen features — but it was so tempting, especially after my air force days. So I did it; and it was tremendously exciting, and a marvelous movie to work on."[[14]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_15-14) Sharp did find shooting footage with old airplanes very slow - "you're fortunate if you can get two set-ups in a day" so when his work was done on *Magnificent Men* he asked his agent to get him any directing job he could. He wound up making a sequel to *The Fly* for Robert Lippert, *[Curse of the Fly](/wiki/Curse_of_the_Fly "Curse of the Fly")* (1965). "I'm afraid they'd pretty much run out of ideas," said Sharp who says he and the writer "both had the feeling,'Oh dear, what a pity they're making another one'."[[30]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_16-30)

Sharp reteamed with Lee for *[The Face of Fu Manchu](/wiki/The_Face_of_Fu_Manchu "The Face of Fu Manchu")* (1965), produced by [Harry Alan Towers](/wiki/Harry_Alan_Towers "Harry Alan Towers") in Ireland. Sharp later said "I like Harry, a great deal... but Harry will get more kick out of making $5 in a slightly crooked and fast way, than he would making $100 legitimately; he's a dealer rather than a movie maker, and he enjoys getting the best part of a deal. But he does have a certain enthusiasm, and a sense of showmanship. In order to make a good film while working with Harry, you have to be insistent."[[30]](#cite_note-Midnight_p_16-30)

*Fu Manchu* was a big hit and led to four sequels; Sharp only directed the first of these, but he worked several more times for Towers who later said he "kept using Don because his films came in on budget and were without exception very successful. On top of that he was a most agreeable person of very good character – no tantrums – clear headed – resourceful; a gentleman too."[[18]](#cite_note-retro-18) The movie would give Sharp a reputation for action movies. He later stated his philosophy:

> You can’t do big action sequences and then have flabby, everyday stuff round it. Those movies have got to have a feeling of latent energy in there... You can’t do action sequences as an entity in themselves. They’ve got to be part of the way a whole movie is developing. You’ve got to have, apart from energy, a very good sense of editing, what a camera can do... a sense of timing... and an ability to have a visual of exactly what it's going to look like... Also, I enjoyed it... some directors... didn’t get the same enjoyment out of it; it was a necessity rather than a pleasure. I always liked doing it, liked doing action.[[18]](#cite_note-retro-18)

Sharp returned to Hammer for *[Rasputin, the Mad Monk](/wiki/Rasputin,_the_Mad_Monk "Rasputin, the Mad Monk")* (1966), with Lee playing the title role. In contrast with *Kiss of the Vampire* and *The Devil Ship Pirates* Sharp said he disliked this experience working for Hammer as the budgets were being tightened.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Sharp made two more films for Towers, *[Our Man in Marrakesh](/wiki/Our_Man_in_Marrakesh "Our Man in Marrakesh")* (1966), a spy spoof starring [Tony Randall](/wiki/Tony_Randall "Tony Randall"), and *[The Brides of Fu Manchu](/wiki/The_Brides_of_Fu_Manchu "The Brides of Fu Manchu")* (1966), again with Lee.[[32]](#cite_note-three-32) After this he worked on an adaptation of [H.G. Wells](/wiki/H.G._Wells "H.G. Wells")' *[The Sleeper Awakes](/wiki/The_Sleeper_Awakes "The Sleeper Awakes")* for [American International Pictures](/wiki/American_International_Pictures "American International Pictures"); Sharp said [Sam Arkoff](/wiki/Sam_Arkoff "Sam Arkoff"), head of AIP ultimately decided not to make this movie because it did "not have enough sex and violence".[[33]](#cite_note-donfive-33)

Sharp then made *[Jules Verne's Rocket to the Moon](/wiki/Jules_Verne%27s_Rocket_to_the_Moon "Jules Verne's Rocket to the Moon")* (1967), an adventure tale in the vein of *Magnificent Men in Their Flying Machines* for Towers. Sharp and Towers were meant to follow this with *Casanova*, a film in the style of *[Tom Jones](/wiki/Tom_Jones_(1963_film) "Tom Jones (1963 film)")* (1963) from a script by [Peter Yeldham](/wiki/Peter_Yeldham "Peter Yeldham") to be shot in [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") starring [Horst Buchholz](/wiki/Horst_Buchholz "Horst Buchholz"). This film was ultimately cancelled due to tensions following the [Six-Day War](/wiki/Six-Day_War "Six-Day War"). Also not made was another proposed Sharp-Towers collaboration, *Legion of the Damned*, based on a script by [Harry Spalding](/wiki/Harry_Spalding "Harry Spalding"), which was to have been shot in Spain; Sharp says Towers was unable to raise the finance, and their collaborations ended.[[33]](#cite_note-donfive-33)

Sharp said "after a couple of months of doing nothing" he returned to TV, directing some episodes of *[The Avengers](/wiki/The_Avengers_(TV_series) "The Avengers (TV series)")* (1968) and *[The Champions](/wiki/The_Champions "The Champions")* (1969).[[33]](#cite_note-donfive-33) He was hired by producer [George Willoughby](/wiki/George_Willoughby_(producer) "George Willoughby (producer)") to direct *[Taste of Excitement](/wiki/Taste_of_Excitement "Taste of Excitement")* (1969), which then led to making *The Violent Enemy* for the same producers (the former would be released first). Sharp was offered *[The Vengeance of She](/wiki/The_Vengeance_of_She "The Vengeance of She")* at Hammer but was unable to take the job.[[34]](#cite_note-Koetting_p_13-34) Sharp was meant to direct the feature film version of *[Till Death Us Do Part](/wiki/Till_Death_Us_Do_Part_(film) "Till Death Us Do Part (film)")* but clashed with [Johnny Speight](/wiki/Johnny_Speight "Johnny Speight") over the script and was fired before filming.[[33]](#cite_note-donfive-33)

### *Puppet on a Chain* and 70s movies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Puppet on a Chain and 70s movies")]

Sharp said he was "out of work for about a year"[[18]](#cite_note-retro-18) when he got an offer to direct a boat chase sequence for *[Puppet on a Chain](/wiki/Puppet_on_a_Chain_(film) "Puppet on a Chain (film)")* (1971), based on a novel by [Alistair MacLean](/wiki/Alistair_MacLean "Alistair MacLean"). The producers liked his work so much they hired him to shoot some additional footage. In 2007 Sharp said the film earned him a reputation as "The Doctor" and he was still getting royalties from the movie.[[18]](#cite_note-retro-18)

Sharp worked on a number of films which did not get made including *Turncoat* from a script by Peter Yeldham, a project with [Judy Geeson](/wiki/Judy_Geeson "Judy Geeson") called *Dead*, and a film in Israel for the producers of *Puppet on a Chain*.[[33]](#cite_note-donfive-33) [Michael Carreras](/wiki/Michael_Carreras "Michael Carreras") of Hammer asked Sharp to take over from [Seth Holt](/wiki/Seth_Holt "Seth Holt") who had died while directing *[Blood from the Mummy's Tomb](/wiki/Blood_from_the_Mummy%27s_Tomb "Blood from the Mummy's Tomb")* (1971) but Sharp was unable as he had a contract to make the aforementioned film in Israel.[[34]](#cite_note-Koetting_p_13-34)[[35]](#cite_note-film-35) According to *Filmink* "it’s a great shame Sharp only worked with" Hammer three times "because he was one of their best ever directors."[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Sharp was put under long-term contract to a company called Scotia who assigned him to direct *[Psychomania](/wiki/Psychomania "Psychomania")* (1973), the final movie of [George Sanders](/wiki/George_Sanders "George Sanders"). This picture has become a cult classic; Sharp called it "great fun to do, especially after doing several films in a row like *The Violent Enemy*. It was a great change, geared for a younger audience as it was."[[37]](#cite_note-37) Scotia loaned out Sharp's services to another company so he could direct *[Dark Places](/wiki/Dark_Places_(1973_film) "Dark Places (1973 film)")* (1973). Sharp then developed further projects with Scotia, and worked for months on another project to be made in Israel; neither was made, nor was a proposed version of the [Robin Hood](/wiki/Robin_Hood "Robin Hood") story.[[38]](#cite_note-donsix-38)

Sharp's next project was *[Callan](/wiki/Callan_(film) "Callan (film)")* (1974), a big screen adaptation of the TV series starring [Edward Woodward](/wiki/Edward_Woodward "Edward Woodward") (1967–72). During the making of that film Sharp received an offer to direct a thriller, *[Hennessy](/wiki/Hennessy_(film) "Hennessy (film)")* (1975), with [Rod Steiger](/wiki/Rod_Steiger "Rod Steiger") in the title role, as an IRA man out to assassinate Queen Elizabeth II.[[38]](#cite_note-donsix-38) This led to Sharp receiving an offer from producer [Harry Saltzman](/wiki/Harry_Saltzman "Harry Saltzman") to work on *The Micronauts*, a "shrunken man" epic to have starred [Gregory Peck](/wiki/Gregory_Peck "Gregory Peck") and [Lee Remick](/wiki/Lee_Remick "Lee Remick").[[39]](#cite_note-Trott_(1975)-39) Sharp worked on the film for months before deciding to leave the project, which was ultimately never made.[[38]](#cite_note-donsix-38)

Michael Carreras offered Sharp the job of directing *[To the Devil a Daughter](/wiki/To_the_Devil_a_Daughter "To the Devil a Daughter")* for Hammer and he was interested but Sharp ultimately pulled out due to dissatisfaction with the script.[[34]](#cite_note-Koetting_p_13-34) Sharp worked on some films that were not made: a proposed film adaptation of Alistair MacLean's *[The Way to Dusty Death](/wiki/The_Way_to_Dusty_Death "The Way to Dusty Death")*; a horror film, *Croc*; an adaptation of [Jeffrey Archer](/wiki/Jeffrey_Archer "Jeffrey Archer")'s *[Not a Penny More, Not a Penny Less](/wiki/Not_a_Penny_More,_Not_a_Penny_Less "Not a Penny More, Not a Penny Less")*; an adaptation of Alistair MacLean's *Bear Island* (originally postponed); a biopic of [Kim Philby](/wiki/Kim_Philby "Kim Philby").[[38]](#cite_note-donsix-38)

Sharp received an offer to direct the fourth version of *[The Four Feathers](/wiki/The_Four_Feathers_(1978_film) "The Four Feathers (1978 film)")* (1978), made for American TV but released theatrically in some markets. He then directed another remake, *[The Thirty Nine Steps](/wiki/The_Thirty_Nine_Steps_(1978_film) "The Thirty Nine Steps (1978 film)")* (1978), with [Robert Powell](/wiki/Robert_Powell "Robert Powell") (who had been in *Four Feathers*).[[40]](#cite_note-timing-40) Greg Smith, who produced *The Thirty Nine Steps*, said he hired Sharp "because he's one of Britain's best action adventure directors and he was familiar with the period."[[41]](#cite_note-new-41) The film was very popular.[[38]](#cite_note-donsix-38)

Eventually the *[Bear Island](/wiki/Bear_Island_(film) "Bear Island (film)")* (1979), project was re-activated and was made starring [Richard Widmark](/wiki/Richard_Widmark "Richard Widmark"), [Donald Sutherland](/wiki/Donald_Sutherland "Donald Sutherland") and [Vanessa Redgrave](/wiki/Vanessa_Redgrave "Vanessa Redgrave"). It was one of the most expensive Canadian films ever made and a box office flop.[[42]](#cite_note-times-42) Following this Sharp was going to make a version of two other MacLean novels - *[Goodbye California](/wiki/Goodbye_California "Goodbye California")*, with [Charlton Heston](/wiki/Charlton_Heston "Charlton Heston"), and *[Air Force One is Down](/wiki/Air_Force_One_is_Down "Air Force One is Down")* - but the finance fell through for both. Neither made were adaptations of *Quicksand* by [Wilfred Greatorex](/wiki/Wilfred_Greatorex "Wilfred Greatorex") and *[Scoop](/wiki/Scoop_(novel) "Scoop (novel)")* by [Evelyn Waugh](/wiki/Evelyn_Waugh "Evelyn Waugh").[[43]](#cite_note-donseven-43)

### Later career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Later career")]

Sharp returned to TV with episodes of *[Hammer House of Horror](/wiki/Hammer_House_of_Horror "Hammer House of Horror")* (1980) ("Guardian of the Abyss") and *[QED](/wiki/Q.E.D._(US_TV_series) "Q.E.D. (US TV series)")* (1982) (TV series).[[34]](#cite_note-Koetting_p_13-34) "It was nice to shoot something again," said Sharp.[[43]](#cite_note-donseven-43)

Sharp developed several projects that were not made - *Spy Ship*; a biopic of [John Simpson Kirkpatrick](/wiki/John_Simpson_Kirkpatrick "John Simpson Kirkpatrick"); *Red Alert West*; a film about the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"). A film that was made was *[What Waits Below](/wiki/What_Waits_Below "What Waits Below")* (1984) shot in America with Robert Powell in the lead role; it was an unhappy experience for Sharp. He developed a film version of [Jack Higgins](/wiki/Jack_Higgins "Jack Higgins")' novel *[A Prayer for the Dying](/wiki/A_Prayer_for_the_Dying "A Prayer for the Dying")* but the eventual movie was directed by Mike Hodges.[[43]](#cite_note-donseven-43) However Sharp was then called in to replace the original director on the mini series *[A Woman of Substance](/wiki/A_Woman_of_Substance_(1985_TV_series) "A Woman of Substance (1985 TV series)")* (1985); based on the novel by [Barbara Taylor Bradford](/wiki/Barbara_Taylor_Bradford "Barbara Taylor Bradford"), and starring [Jenny Seagrove](/wiki/Jenny_Seagrove "Jenny Seagrove") and [Deborah Kerr](/wiki/Deborah_Kerr "Deborah Kerr"). This was a huge ratings success.[[43]](#cite_note-donseven-43)

After *[Tusitala](/wiki/Tusitala_(miniseries) "Tusitala (miniseries)")* (1986), an Australian mini series shot in Samoa, Sharp directed *[Hold the Dream](/wiki/Hold_the_Dream "Hold the Dream")* (1986), a mini-series sequel to *Woman of Substance*, with Jenny Seagrove reprising her role. *[Tears in the Rain](/wiki/Tears_in_the_Rain "Tears in the Rain")* (1988) was a TV movie from a novel by [Pamela Wallace](/wiki/Pamela_Wallace "Pamela Wallace") which gave an early starring role to [Sharon Stone](/wiki/Sharon_Stone "Sharon Stone"). *[Act of Will](/wiki/Act_of_Will "Act of Will")* (1989) was another mini series based on a novel by [Barbara Taylor Bradford](/wiki/Barbara_Taylor_Bradford "Barbara Taylor Bradford"), which starred [Liz Hurley](/wiki/Liz_Hurley "Liz Hurley").[[44]](#cite_note-doneight-44)

## Personal life

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Personal life")]

Sharp married Australian actress Gwenda Wilson in 1945, after appearing on stage with her in *Kiss and Tell*.[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-WA-46) In 1956, he married actress Mary Steele who he had met while shooting a documentary, *Crossroads*.[[15]](#cite_note-dontwo-15)

Sharp died on 14 December 2011, after a short spell in hospital.[[3]](#cite_note-ind-3) He was survived by Mary Steele,[[47]](#cite_note-47) two sons and a daughter. Another son, [Massive Attack](/wiki/Massive_Attack "Massive Attack") producer [Jonny Dollar](/wiki/Jonny_Dollar "Jonny Dollar"), predeceased him in 2009.

## Theatre credits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Don_Sharp&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Theatre credits")]
